summary: Rev. Jedson White organized [[gap_st_john_s_baptist_church|St. John's Baptist Church]] in Brooksville, Oklahoma, in 1906 and promoted the all-Black town to African Americans across the South, contributing to its early growth and community development during the Jim Crow era.

claims.object: St. John's Baptist Church in Brooksville, Oklahoma
Brooksville as a destination for African American settlement
an all-Black town during the Jim Crow era

historicalContext: Brooksville was one of over fifty all-Black towns established in Oklahoma during the early 20th century, many as responses to racial violence and segregation in the South. These towns represented acts of Black self-governance and resistance to disenfranchisement, enabled by available land and the promise of autonomy, though many later declined due to economic pressures and migration.
